Name: quantization_with_information_bottleneck
Title: Quantization Optimization via Information Bottleneck Theory
Short Hypothesis: By framing quantization as an information bottleneck problem, we can derive a novel quantization strategy that minimizes task-relevant distortion while ensuring a provable bound on the bits-per-weight ratio, leading to efficient model compression.
Related Work: Existing literature, such as the work by Gao et al. (2018), has explored rate-distortion limits for model compression, while Young (2025) has discussed quantization from an RD perspective. This proposal distinguishes itself by explicitly framing quantization within the information bottleneck context, allowing us to derive operational limits that can be directly tested and validated.
Abstract: This research proposes a novel quantization strategy for model compression by framing it through the lens of information bottleneck theory. Our central hypothesis is that by optimizing the quantization process to minimize task-relevant distortion while adhering to a particular bits-per-weight constraint, we can achieve superior model compression without significant performance degradation. We will derive explicit bounds on the achievable rate-distortion trade-offs for quantization schemes and validate these theoretically through minimax arguments. Furthermore, we will implement a quantization algorithm that leverages these findings, allowing for dynamic adjustment of quantization levels based on the task requirements. This presents a significant advancement over existing methods that often lack a theoretical underpinning. Our approach will be empirically evaluated against standard benchmarks to demonstrate its effectiveness in preserving predictive accuracy while achieving substantial compression.
Experiments: Derive theoretical bounds on the achievable rate-distortion trade-offs for quantization schemes based on the information bottleneck framework.
Implement a quantization algorithm that dynamically adjusts quantization levels based on task requirements, and compare its performance to existing quantization techniques.
Evaluate the proposed method on standard benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) to assess both the compression rates and accuracy retention against baseline models.
Use metrics such as bits-per-weight, accuracy, and distortion rates to quantify the trade-offs and validate the theoretical predictions.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential limitations include the complexity of deriving exact theoretical bounds, which may require simplifying assumptions. Moreover, the performance of the proposed quantization method may vary across different model architectures and tasks, necessitating careful evaluation.
